sources.notes: Statement and constraints: permutation, delete median of any length-3 subarray, answer independently for every original position.
Official editorial gives the extrema-reduction invariant, answer range 2/3/4/5, and sparse-table implementation over reverse/value-complement symmetries.
Luogu solution article used to cross-check the high-level classification for answers <=4 and <=3; some symmetric cases are intentionally omitted in prose.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution index; the article listed in sources was the actually consulted Luogu source.

annotation.summary: For each element of a permutation, find the minimum remaining length of an obtainable array that still contains that element when each operation deletes the median of a chosen triple.
annotation.constraints: Sum n <= 2e5, so all per-index answers must be computed in O(n log n) or O(n) total per test.
annotation.core_idea: Global minimum 1 and maximum n can never be deleted. Any fixed interval can be reduced to only its minimum and maximum, so every non-extreme element has answer between 3 and 5. For each i, prefix/suffix minimum and maximum positions around i decide whether one side-extreme can be eliminated, giving answer <=4. Then deciding whether the final array can be exactly [1,p_i,n] requires considering the relative order of pos_1, pos_n, i and the side extrema. The official implementation applies reverse and value-complement symmetries so one canonical case covers all orientations, and uses range min/max queries over critical intervals to distinguish answer 3 from 4 in the remaining cases.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) with sparse tables as in the editorial implementation.
tricks: reduce any interval to its min and max
answers collapse to 2 for global extrema and 3/4/5 otherwise
apply reverse and value-complement symmetries to avoid duplicating cases
use range min/max to test whether a helper outside the endpoint value interval exists

solution_variants.name: extrema-classification-sparse-table
solution_variants.summary: Precompute prefix/suffix extrema positions plus range min/max sparse tables. Classify each i under canonical orientation and its reverse/value-complement symmetries, improving default answer 5 to 4 or 3 when the extrema configuration permits.

solution_variants.name: linear-extrema-classification
solution_variants.summary: The same classification can be implemented in O(n) with specialized preprocessing for the needed min/max witnesses instead of generic sparse tables.

tags.evidence: The solution starts from the fact that global 1 and n are undeletable and every interval can be reduced to its minimum and maximum.
For every i, the answer is determined by prefix min/max positions before i and suffix min/max positions after i, plus range extrema inside critical intervals.
The core difficulty is the exhaustive classification of whether the answer is 3, 4, or 5 under the relative orders of pos_1, pos_n, i, and side extrema.
The official implementation uses static range minimum and maximum queries to test critical intervals for helper values.
Reverse order and value-complement transformations reduce mirrored cases to one canonical classifier.
Undeletability of global extrema and reducibility of an interval to its two extrema are invariants used to bound and classify answers.
